questions.text: If a text contains the use of humour, irony, exaggeration or ridicule to expose and criticize people's stupidity or vices, it can be referred to as an example of _.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Fiction: Fiction refers to literature created from the imagination, not necessarily focusing on critiquing societal vices or stupidity through humor, irony, exaggeration, or ridicule. While satire can be a form of fiction, not all fiction is satirical.

Comic: While comedy often involves humor, it may not necessarily aim to criticize or expose societal vices or stupidity. Comedy can be lighthearted and entertaining without necessarily having the critical or moral undertones that satire typically has.

Tragedy: Tragedy is a genre of literature that typically involves serious and somber themes, often focusing on the downfall of the protagonist due to their own flaws or external circumstances. Tragedy usually lacks the satirical elements present in works aiming to criticize societal vices or stupidity through humor, irony, exaggeration, or ridicule.
Satire uses humor, irony, exaggeration, or ridicule to criticize people's flaws, vices, or shortcomings. It's a genre that aims to expose and critique something through humor or mockery.

questions.explanation: Satire uses humor, irony, exaggeration, or ridicule to criticize people's flaws, vices, or shortcomings. It's a genre that aims to expose and critique something through humor or mockery.

questions.text: Gulrukh always does her job in time. The underlined word is an example of _.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Anaphoric reference refers to a word or phrase in a text that refers back to another word or phrase used earlier in the text. In this case, "her" refers back to "Gulrukh," which was mentioned earlier in the sentence.

Cataphoric reference: Cataphoric reference is when a word or phrase refers to something mentioned later in the text. In the sentence provided, "her" is not referring to anything that comes after it; instead, it refers back to "Gulrukh," which was mentioned earlier in the sentence. Therefore, cataphoric reference is not applicable here.

Cross-reference: Cross-reference typically involves referring to another part of the text, a different text, or an external source for additional information or clarification. In the given sentence, "her" is not making a reference to any other part of the text or an external source; it simply refers back to "Gulrukh" mentioned earlier. Thus, cross-reference is not relevant in this context.

Historical reference: Historical reference involves making a reference to a historical event, figure, or context. The given sentence does not contain any reference to a historical event or figure; it simply describes Gulrukh's punctuality. Therefore, historical reference is not applicable here.

questions.explanation: Anaphoric reference refers to a word or phrase in a text that refers back to another word or phrase used earlier in the text. In this case, "her" refers back to "Gulrukh," which was mentioned earlier in the sentence.

questions.text: Which one of the following sentence contains the transitional device of consequence?
questions.subject: English

options.text: Saad burnt midnight oil but could not get as many marks as he desired.
options.explanation: This sentence presents a contrast between Saad's effort (burning midnight oil) and the outcome (not achieving the desired marks). It doesn't specifically indicate consequence but rather contrasts actions or situations.

options.text: Hasnain would have attended the party had he been invited.
options.explanation: This sentence presents a hypothetical situation using the conditional "would have," suggesting what would have happened under a different circumstance. It doesn't directly indicate consequence but rather presents a hypothetical scenario.

options.text: Resultantly, he won the hearts of his subordinates.
options.explanation: In this sentence, the word "resultantly" indicates a consequence or result of a preceding action or situation. It serves as a transitional device to show the outcome of something that has occurred previously.

options.text: In comparison to India, Pakistan is a much more responsible nuclear state.
options.explanation: This sentence establishes a comparison between India and Pakistan regarding their status as nuclear states. It doesn't convey consequence but rather highlights a comparison between two entities.

questions.explanation: In this sentence, the word "resultantly" indicates a consequence or result of a preceding action or situation. It serves as a transitional device to show the outcome of something that has occurred previously.

questions.text: If a wolf refuses to help its pack hunt, it becomes an outcast and must go on alone. Which one of the following is the meaning of the underlined word?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Pariah refers to someone who is excluded or ostracized from a group or society. This perfectly matches the situation of the wolf being rejected by its pack.
favourite: A favourite is someone or something that is liked the most. An outcast is the opposite, being someone who is rejected or excluded.
messenger: A messenger is someone who delivers a message. While a wolf might be sent away, being an outcast implies a broader rejection from the pack, not just a specific task.
flighter: "Flighter" isn't a real word, and the context suggests a more permanent state of being excluded, not just someone who flees.

questions.explanation: Pariah refers to someone who is excluded or ostracized from a group or society. This perfectly matches the situation of the wolf being rejected by its pack.

questions.text: The third person omniscient point of view is the most objective and trustworthy because:
questions.subject: English

options.text: an all-knowing narrator is telling the story.
options.explanation: The closest reason to why third person omniscient might be seen as objective and trustworthy is:a- an all-knowing narrator is telling the story.

options.text: the narrator can easily be identified.
options.explanation: the narrator can easily be identified: In third person omniscient, the narrator is not a character and remains hidden, not easily identified.
the characters appear familiar: There's no guarantee characters will feel familiar in any point of view.

options.text: the events are in chronological order.
options.explanation: the events are in chronological order: While some omniscient narrators might present events chronologically, it's not a requirement. They can jump around in time.

questions.explanation: An omniscient narrator has access to the thoughts and feelings of all the characters, and potentially even knows the story's entire outcome. This ability to see the "big picture" can create a sense of objectivity and trustworthiness, as the reader gets a broader perspective that isn't limited to any one character's viewpoint

questions.text: 'As the doctor examined the patient and his reports, signs of worry appeared on his face'. From this, we can infer that the patient, most probably, _.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: going to die: While the doctor's worry could indicate a very serious condition, the sentence doesn't provide enough information to jump to such a conclusion.

The doctor's worry suggests the patient's condition is concerning, but it doesn't give enough details to pinpoint the exact issue or severity. "Has some serious problem" captures the idea of a significant issue requiring attention without going to the extremes of the other options.
needs amputation: This is a specific possibility, but the sentence doesn't mention anything suggesting such a drastic measure.
will take years to recover: The sentence suggests a problem, but the timeframe for recovery is not implied.

questions.explanation: The doctor's worry suggests the patient's condition is concerning, but it doesn't give enough details to pinpoint the exact issue or severity. "Has some serious problem" captures the idea of a significant issue requiring attention without going to the extremes of the other options.

questions.text: The bridal carriage was decorated from stem to stern. The underlined idiomatic expression means:
questions.subject: English

options.text: all the way from front to back
options.explanation: "Stem to stern" is a nautical term referring to the front and back of a ship. When used figuratively, it means decorating the entire length or extent of something, which perfectly describes the bridal carriage.

options.text: from the front and from the back
options.explanation: from the front and from the back: While the decoration might be visible from both sides, the idiom emphasizes that the entire carriage, from one end to the other, is decorated.
with sharp and shiny colours: The idiom doesn't specify the colors or their shininess. It focuses on the extent of the decoration.

options.text: in a very simple but impressive way
options.explanation: in a very simple but impressive way: The idiom is purely about the location of the decoration, not the style.

questions.explanation: "Stem to stern" is a nautical term referring to the front and back of a ship. When used figuratively, it means decorating the entire length or extent of something, which perfectly describes the bridal carriage.

questions.text: Which one of the following sentence exemplifies the use of oxymoron?
questions.subject: English

options.text: I have told you a million times not to touch my stuff.
options.explanation: I have told you a million times not to touch my stuff. This is an exaggeration, but "million times" is not a concept with inherently opposing meanings.

options.text: Zain's faith is as strong as a rock.
options.explanation: Zain's faith is as strong as a rock. This is a simile, comparing faith to the strength of a rock. It doesn't contain contradictory terms.

The mountains sing together. This uses personification, giving mountains the ability to sing. While it might be a fantastical image, it doesn't have conflicting meanings within a single word or phrase.

options.text: Good night! Parting is such a sweet sorrow.
options.explanation: An oxymoron combines opposite meanings within a word or phrase. In "Parting is such a sweet sorrow," parting is usually associated with sadness, while sweet suggests something pleasant. This combination of opposing meanings creates the oxymoron.

questions.explanation: An oxymoron combines opposite meanings within a word or phrase. In "Parting is such a sweet sorrow," parting is usually associated with sadness, while sweet suggests something pleasant. This combination of opposing meanings creates the oxymoron.

questions.text: ‘If I were you, I would not disrespect my teacher just for asking me to rewrite the assignment.’ The implied meaning of the sentence is
questions.subject: English

options.text: I am extraordinarily respectful to my teachers.
options.explanation: I am extraordinarily respectful to my teachers: The sentence focuses on the general advice, not the speaker's personal behavior.

options.text: My teachers are more respectable than yours.
options.explanation: My teachers are more respectable than yours: There's no comparison between teachers here. The focus is on respecting teachers in general.
Teachers should demand respect: While the sentence implies respecting teachers, it doesn't explicitly say they should demand it.

options.text: I advise you not to be disrespectful to your teachers.
options.explanation: The entire structure "If I were you..." suggests offering advice. The specific content of the advice (not disrespecting teachers for asking for a rewrite) points towards d as the implied meaning.

questions.explanation: The entire structure "If I were you..." suggests offering advice. The specific content of the advice (not disrespecting teachers for asking for a rewrite) points towards d as the implied meaning.

questions.text: Which one of the following sentence contains both a fact and an opinion?
questions.subject: English

options.text: Pakistan came into being in 1947 and the Quaid-e-Azam became the first governor general.
Mangoes are delicious, but apples are not.
options.explanation: This sentence expresses an opinion about their taste.

options.text: The horse is an animal riding which is an exciting experience.
options.explanation: In sentence (c), "The horse is an animal" is a fact, while "riding which is an exciting experience" expresses an opinion. Although the sentence structure combines them, it does include both a fact and an opinion.

options.text: Islam is one of the Abrahamic religions and so is Christianity.
options.explanation: Islam is one of the Abrahamic religions and so is Christianity. This sentence presents two facts about religious classifications. There's no opinion involved.

questions.explanation: In sentence (c), "The horse is an animal" is a fact, while "riding which is an exciting experience" expresses an opinion. Although the sentence structure combines them, it does include both a fact and an opinion.

questions.text: Laws were quickly passed so that the child abuse could be checked. The underlined part of the sentence is a/an:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: noun clause: Noun clauses typically function as subjects or objects in a sentence. Here, the underlined part doesn't act as either.
adjectival clause: Adjectival clauses modify nouns or pronouns. The underlined part doesn't modify any noun or pronoun in this sentence.

Adverbial clauses function like adverbs, modifying verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. In this case, the underlined clause modifies the verb "passed" by explaining the purpose of the law being passed. It answers the question "why" the laws were passed. So, "so that the child abuse could be checked" acts as an adverbial clause of purpose.
independent clause: An independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. The underlined part relies on the main clause ("Laws were quickly passed") for its meaning and cannot function alone.

questions.explanation: Adverbial clauses function like adverbs, modifying verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. In this case, the underlined clause modifies the verb "passed" by explaining the purpose of the law being passed. It answers the question "why" the laws were passed. So, "so that the child abuse could be checked" acts as an adverbial clause of purpose.

questions.text: Jupiter is _ the solar system. Which one of the following preposition will correctly fill in the blank?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Jupiter is in the solar system" is a grammatically correct sentence. "In" can be used to indicate location within a larger container or area. The solar system can be considered a vast space containing Jupiter and other celestial bodies.
on: "On" typically refers to a surface. While planets orbit "on" their axis, they don't physically reside "on" the solar system in the same way they might be on a surface.
Of the solar system" emphasizes Jupiter's membership or belonging as a part of the solar system.
into: "Into" suggests movement towards something. Jupiter isn't moving into the solar system, it's already a part of it.

questions.explanation: "Jupiter is in the solar system" is a grammatically correct sentence. "In" can be used to indicate location within a larger container or area. The solar system can be considered a vast space containing Jupiter and other celestial bodies.

questions.text: I have given up the habit of reading aloud. The underlined part is a/an _ phrase.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: participle: Participles are verb forms that function as adjectives. In this case, "given up" is a past participle verb form acting as a verb phrase with "have" to indicate a completed action.
preposition: Prepositions show relationships between words or phrases. "Of" shows the relationship between "habit" and "reading aloud."
﻿infinitive: Infinitives are verb forms that can function as nouns, adjectives, or adverbs. Here, there's no "to" before "reading aloud," so it's not an infinitive.
Gerunds are verb forms that function as nouns. Here, "reading aloud" acts as a noun phrase referring to the action (habit) that has been given up. It describes the specific habit being abandoned.

questions.explanation: Gerunds are verb forms that function as nouns. Here, "reading aloud" acts as a noun phrase referring to the action (habit) that has been given up. It describes the specific habit being abandoned.

questions.text: Kiran‟s belief that she would succeed one day cheered her up. The underlined part is a/an_.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: noun phrase: A noun phrase typically includes a noun and any modifiers (articles, adjectives) around it. Here, "belief" is the noun, but "that she would succeed one day" provides more information about the belief, functioning as a dependent clause.

Noun clauses function as nouns in a sentence. In this case, "that she would succeed one day" acts as the object of the preposition "of" and explains what Kiran believes. It provides the content of her belief, making it a noun clause.
adjective phase: An adjective phrase modifies a noun or pronoun. There's no noun or pronoun being modified by "that she would succeed one day" in this sentence.

adjective clause: An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun, but it uses a relative pronoun (who, which, that) to connect to the noun it modifies. Here, "that" functions as a subordinating conjunction to introduce the dependent clause, not a relative pronoun connecting back to "Kiran."

questions.explanation: Noun clauses function as nouns in a sentence. In this case, "that she would succeed one day" acts as the object of the preposition "of" and explains what Kiran believes. It provides the content of her belief, making it a noun clause.

questions.text: One often asks _ what will come next. Which one of the following pronouns will best fill in the blank?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Oneself is a reflexive pronoun that refers back to a singular indefinite pronoun like "one" in a general sense. It emphasizes that the question of "what will come next" is something a person ponders about themself.
himself: This pronoun is masculine and singular, which wouldn't be universally applicable to "one."
herself: This pronoun is feminine and singular, similar to "himself," it's not universally applicable.
themselves: This pronoun is plural and reflexive, implying multiple people asking about their own futures.

questions.explanation: Oneself is a reflexive pronoun that refers back to a singular indefinite pronoun like "one" in a general sense. It emphasizes that the question of "what will come next" is something a person ponders about themself.

questions.text: ‘They asked him how he got injured, but he refused to answer.’ This is a/an _ sentence.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: interrogative: Interrogative sentences ask a question. While the first part ("They asked him how he got injured") is a question, the sentence continues with another clause ("but he refused to answer").

simple: Simple sentences contain only one independent clause (a clause with a subject and verb that can stand alone as a complete sentence). This sentence has two independent clauses ("They asked..." and "he refused...").

compound: Compound sentences contain two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction (like for, but, and, or, nor, so, yet). This sentence has a dependent clause ("how he got injured") introduced by the subordinating conjunction "how."
Compound-complex sentences combine the features of compound and complex sentences. They have two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction, and at least one dependent clause.

questions.explanation: Compound-complex sentences combine the features of compound and complex sentences. They have two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction, and at least one dependent clause.

questions.text: Fatima wanted to overcome her fears. The underlined part of sentence is a/an _ infinitive.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple infinitive is the base form of a verb preceded by the word "to." In this case, "to overcome" is the simple infinitive form of the verb "overcome." It functions as the object of the verb "wanted" and expresses the action that Fatima desired to perform.

Gerundial: A gerundial infinitive would involve the use of the gerund form of the verb (the -ing form) as the object of the verb "wanted." For example, "Fatima wanted overcoming her fears." However, this construction doesn't make sense in English, as "overcoming" is not functioning as a gerund here but as a simple infinitive.

Bare: A bare infinitive would involve using the base form of the verb without the "to" particle. For example, "Fatima wanted overcome her fears." However, in standard English, after the verb "wanted," we use the "to" particle followed by the base form of the verb, making it a simple infinitive.

Split: A split infinitive occurs when an adverb or other word is inserted between "to" and the verb in an infinitive form. For example, "Fatima wanted to quickly overcome her fears." In the given sentence, there is no adverb or other word splitting the infinitive "to overcome." It remains in its simple form

questions.explanation: A simple infinitive is the base form of a verb preceded by the word "to." In this case, "to overcome" is the simple infinitive form of the verb "overcome." It functions as the object of the verb "wanted" and expresses the action that Fatima desired to perform.

questions.text: _ money I have will suffice for my present needs. Which one of the following will correctly fill in the blank?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A little is the most natural way to express an indefinite small amount of money that will still be enough for current needs. "A little" functions as a determiner indicating the amount.
an little: This is grammatically incorrect. Articles (a/an/the) are used before nouns. "Little" is an adjective, so "an" wouldn't be used in this way.
the little: "The" implies a specific amount of money already mentioned in context. Here, the amount is indefinite.
little: By itself, "little" can be an adjective modifying a noun (e.g., "little money") but it cannot function alone as a determiner before a noun like "money" in this case.

questions.explanation: A little is the most natural way to express an indefinite small amount of money that will still be enough for current needs. "A little" functions as a determiner indicating the amount.

questions.text: Which one of the following conditional sentences is correctly structured?
questions.subject: English

options.text: Had he been wise, he would not have done such foolish things.
options.explanation: A. Had he been wise, he would not have done such foolish things is indeed a correctly structured conditional sentence.

options.text: Had he been wise, he would not do such foolish things.
options.explanation: Had he been wise, he would not do such foolish things: As mentioned before, this mixes verb tenses. "Had" suggests a past action, but "would not do" is present tense. The consequence in a past unreal conditional should also be past tense.

options.text: Were he wise, he would not have done such foolish things.
options.explanation: Were he wise: This uses the subjunctive mood for the hypothetical condition, but the consequence clause ("would not have done") is still past perfect tense, which is used in past unreal conditionals. While the subjunctive mood might be used in some variations of past unreal conditionals, it's not the most common structure.

options.text: Was he wise, he would not have done such foolish things.
options.explanation: Was he wise, he would not have done such foolish things: This lacks the subjunctive mood ("were") typically used in past unreal conditionals.

questions.explanation: A. Had he been wise, he would not have done such foolish things is indeed a correctly structured conditional sentence.

questions.text: I consider Muneeb my best friend. The verb used in the sentence is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: mono-transitive: This term is not commonly used. Transitive verbs can have one or two objects, so "mono-transitive" isn't necessary to describe a verb that takes one object.
di-transitive: Di-transitive verbs take two objects, a direct object and an indirect object. In this sentence, "consider" only has one object ("Muneeb").

Transitive verbs require a direct object to complete their meaning. In "I consider Muneeb my best friend," "Muneeb" is the direct object receiving the action of the verb "consider." This completes the meaning of the sentence, making "consider" a transitive verb in this context.
intransitive: Intransitive verbs do not take a direct object. While "consider" can be used intransitively (e.g., "I considered for a moment"), in this sentence it has an object ("Muneeb").

questions.explanation: Transitive verbs require a direct object to complete their meaning. In "I consider Muneeb my best friend," "Muneeb" is the direct object receiving the action of the verb "consider." This completes the meaning of the sentence, making "consider" a transitive verb in this context.

questions.text: Read the given passages and answer any SIX questions including Question No.(i) appended to it.Khalid Bin al-Waleed who had not yet converted to Islam noticed that the strategic pass to the left of the army of the Muslims was unguarded. He immediately seized the opportunity to attack, still at the pass, with his cavalry. The pickets fought bravely but all of them including Abdullah ibn Jubair were overpowered and martyred. Khalid ibn al- Waleed captured the pass and attacked the army of the Muslims from the rear.The Muslim army was busy in gathering Mal-e-Ghanimat, utterly oblivious of everything else. Suddenly, they were startled by the charge of enemy's cavalry in its rear. Abu Sufyan also noticed the manoeuver of Khalid, and the bewilderment of the Muslims. He rallied his troops, returned to the scene of action and launched a frontal attack upon them. The Muslims found themselves caught in a pincer movement of the enemy, and they panicked. In a short time the tables were turned on them, and victory was wrenched out of their hands.The Prophet (PBUH) had given the banner of Islam to Masaab ibn Umayer (R.A). He was martyred by the enemy and the banner of Islam fell on the ground. But Hazrat Ali (R.A) seized the sacred banner and bore it aloft amidst the storm of battle. As Masaab resembled the Prophet (PBUH) in person, a shout was put up by the enemy that Hazrat Muhammed (PBUH) God forbid, was martyred. The morale of the polytheists of Makkah was beefed up by this shout but demoralized Muslim army.The beloved Rasool (PBUH) was fighting in the other part of the battlefield, had been wounded, and his head and holy face were bleeding. A few Muslims mainly the Ansar, were defending him. It was this little group and its battle cries that caught Ali's (R.A) attention. He tore his way through the enemy lines and came up to his comrades-in-arms. They stood surrounding the Rasool (PBUH), led by Abu Dujana (R.A). They were doing their best to shield him from the weapons of the enemy. He was thrilled to see his master alive.QUESTIONS:Write down summary of the passage and suggest a suitable title. (7+1=8)What was the manoeuver of Khalid ibn al-Waleed which startled the Muslims?How did Abu Sufyan exploit the situation?What was the incident which prompted the spread of the rumour of the HolyProphet (PBUH) being martyred?“He tore his way through the enemy lines.” What does it tells us about the braveryand the battle skills of Hazrat Ali (R.A)?„The Ansar (R.A) protected the Holy Prophet (PBUH) from the enemy at the riskof their own lives.‟ How is it a great lesson for us?Why were the Muslims demoralized at the rumour of the Holy Prophet (PBUH)being martyred? What was the effect of this rumour on the fighting polytheists?Hazrat Khalid ibn al-Waleed was a smart and skillful warrior even before embracing Islam. What light does the above cited incident throw on him as a military commander?
questions.subject: English
questions.explanation: Explanation will be added later
